I am not sure about the word `strange` you mean here.

Here is the concept of OOP:
```python
class Parent(object):
    def say(self):
        self.word = "Hello"

class Child(Parent):
    def say(self):
        self.word = "Hello World!!"

parent = Parent()
parent.say()
child = Child()
child.say()
```
* Why child can call `say` directly?
Because child defines its own function with same name in parent, but there is no relation between both.

---

However, the case might be different if there is some relations between Parent and Child:
```python
class Parent(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.say_cnt = 0
    def say(self):
        self.word = "Hello"
        self.say_cnt += 1

class Child(Parent):
    def say(self):
        self.word = "Hello World!!"

parent = Parent()
parent.say()
print(parent.say_cnt) #1

child = Child()
child.say()
print(child.say_cnt) #0
```

---

In this case, Child will not call parent's `say`, but for achieving normal action on `say_cnt` in Parent, we can use `super(Child, self).say()` in Child.

```python
class Child(Parent):
    def say(self):
        super(Child, self).say()
        self.word = "Hello World!!"
```

Great that it's already possible. The main problem might be lack of a "how to get positions in my ast" guide?

I typically don't use automagic AST generators, since the structure of the parsed text normally does not match the structure of my AST. As a simple example, consider
``` text
vardef: VAR type variables SEMICOL ;
type: NAME ;
variables: NAME | variables COMMA NAME ;
NAME: /[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9]+/;
```
This declares variables in C style, ie `int i, j, k;` In my AST, I'd like
``` python
t = Type('int', intPos)
AST = [VarDecl(t, Variable('i', iPos)),
       VarDecl(t, Variable("j", jPos)),
       VarDecl(t, Variable("k", kPos))]
```
This makes further processing of variable declarations easier. Automagic AST generation can't handle such structural changes, for the simple reason that it doesn't know what I want.

As above, let's not raise the socket module's exception. We should use haigha's own exception. The problem here is that the higher level module (and the engineer who is debugging) doesn't get an opportunity to find out the underlying error and error message that may be critical for resolving the issue.

A good approach may be to track how many have been tried, and if we find ourselves in the `except socket.error` block having just tried the last address, simply re-raise that last exception directly, preserving the traceback, socket.error.errno, and error message.

Logging the excpetion values would be extremely helpful here, too: perhaps at debug level for intermediate failures and at error level for the final failure (when there are no more addresses to try)

Microsoft have pulled away from inline assembler and have moved to compiler intrinsics (https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/26td21ds.aspx). This cpuid guarded section shows the use of that. To use the assembler on all platforms would require this asm to be separated out into a file and the build updated to assemble them. Or, leave this in place for almost all platforms, and either leave intrinsics in place or extract into an asm file for Windows builds.

It's not possible to set these attributes unconditionally because if you do, then pickling the python classes fails: `Pickler.save_global` raises an exception when it finds out that `getattr(module, FooPy.__name__)` (where `FooPy.__name__` has been set to `Foo`) is not the same thing as `getattr(module, 'Foo')`.  This occurs when both the C and Python classes are present and you try to pickle the Python class; maybe this is a corner case, but it arises in the unit tests, and would arise in fixing a corrupted database and maybe elsewhere.

Nitpick: Consider changing this to:

```python
expected = set()
for line in f.read().splitlines():
    line = line.strip()
    if line and not line.startswith('#'):

```

This will have the following effects:

1. Blank lines will be skipped (important if a future committer has an editor that automatically adds blank lines to the end of the file, as many do).

2. Any line starting with `#` will be skipped, allowing comments to exist in the baseline file if needed in the future.

this is insignificant here, but, generally, prefix increment is preferred in C++ for performance reasons. Quoting:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3181211/prefix-postfix-increment-operators
"It is typical that a postfix operator will be worse on performance because you have to create another copy before doing the increment (and this is why I've gotten in the habit of always using prefix unless I need something else)."
